prompt: Close-up portrait, dramatic chiaroscuro studio lighting, of a craftsman seated at a wooden workbench. Where his hands and forearms should be, there are instead beautiful brass and steel measuring instruments — calipers, rulers, micrometers, gauges, set squares — organically fused where flesh would be, as if they grew there or were always there. The instruments are immaculate, perfectly calibrated, glinting in the light. They are posed as hands would be — reaching toward the work. Before him on the bench sits a raw piece of wood, untouched, unworked, waiting. Wood shavings from someone else's work litter the bench around it. His face is calm, focused, expert — the expression of a master at work. But nothing is being made. The instruments cannot hold a chisel. The measure has become the hand. The craftsman does not appear distressed — he appears to be working, optimizing, measuring — and that is the horror. Photorealistic, not surreal or fantastical in tone. The wrongness comes entirely from the substitution, not from dreamlike distortion. Warm amber workshop light, deep shadows, rich wood grain texture. No text, no labels, no numbers, no annotations.
visual_logic: A craftsman whose hands have become measuring instruments — calipers, rulers, gauges — perfectly calibrated and idle, while the raw unworked wood sits untouched before him. The measure has replaced the maker.
